finding: Initial test inputs were code files (TypeScript, YAML, TSX), but craft-reflect's declared scope is 'prompt or skill' artifacts.
consequence: A poor baseline would have been unattributable — skill weakness vs scope mismatch would be indistinguishable.
action: Replaced inputs with scope-appropriate artifacts: a vague prompt, craft-blueprint SKILL.md, craft-prompt SKILL.md.
rationale: An autoresearch baseline is only trustworthy when inputs match the target skill's declared scope. This kind of mismatch is the first thing to check before any metric is meaningful.

finding: Baseline scored 9/9 (100%) on the binary eval suite. Qualitative inspection of the three outputs reveals real quality gaps the binary suite does not measure: Recommended-changes and Issues are 1:1 mapped (no prioritization); Minimal-rewrite-plan is a subset of Recommended-changes rather than an ordered sequence; Failure-modes frequently restate Issues; no severity labels.
consequence: Mutating against a saturated eval suite would produce noise. The loop cannot learn when all signal is at ceiling.
action: Before running any mutation, extend the eval suite with assertions that target the gaps: (E5) Recommended-changes items are at a different granularity or consolidation level from Issues; (E6) Minimal-rewrite-plan is an ordered, prioritized sequence, not a subset of Recommended-changes; (E7) Failure-modes names distinct dimensions, not future-tense rephrasings of Issues; (E8) an explicit severity or priority signal is present in Issues.
rationale: This is the 'false positive' scenario documented in references/eval-guide.md § 'When the scores look fine but real use does not.' Validates that the false-positive guidance is correct — but also flags that the eval suite authored up-front was too shape-focused. A future refinement to eval-guide.md should warn that a near-100% baseline on the first pass usually means the suite is too loose, not that the skill is perfect.
